naturalLanguage: Pacific salmon (genus Oncorhynchus) are animals — anadromous fish born in fresh water, maturing in the ocean, and returning to their birth rivers to spawn. SUMO's Animal classification correctly captures their biological category.
Salmon fishing in the Salish Sea is a food production process — a systematic method for harvesting wild protein for immediate consumption and long-term storage.

Reef net fishing is a heritage skill — a sophisticated cooperative technology developed by Lummi (Lhaq'temish) and WSÁNEĆ (Saanich) peoples for the specific reef channel conditions of the San Juan and Gulf Islands. The multi-family coordination protocols and ecological knowledge of reef geography are integral to the skill.

Reef net fishing is an intentional process — a deliberate sequence of positioning canoes, setting the net, reading water signs for approaching salmon schools, signaling, and raising the net in coordination.

Salmon smoking is a food preservation process — the controlled application of smoke and heat to salmon fillets to reduce moisture content, introduce preservative compounds, and achieve a minimum safe internal temperature of 160°F (71°C).
Salmon smoking is also a heritage skill encoding generations of knowledge about alder wood selection, smoke temperature management, fish preparation, and storage conditions.

The First Salmon Ceremony is a cultural practice — a structured communal response to the arrival of the first salmon of the season, encoding the relationship of reciprocal obligation between human communities and the Salmon People.
River weir construction is a manufacturing process — the systematic placement of wooden stakes and woven barriers to create a salmon trap in a river channel.

River weir construction is also a heritage skill — the knowledge of which rivers support weir placement, how to read water depth and current for optimal trap placement, and how to leave passage for spawning fish.
